narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Monday, May 18, 2026

First Warn Headlines

Multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms are expected through much of the week.
A few storms today and Tuesday may become severe.
Heavy rainfall and localized flash flooding remain concerns, especially across central Missouri.
Damaging winds, hail, and isolated tornadoes are possible through Tuesday evening.
Cooler weather arrives midweek, but additional storm chances may linger into late week and next weekend.

An active and unsettled weather pattern will continue across Missouri and Illinois this week as several disturbances move through the central United States. While the most significant severe weather concerns remain focused on today and Tuesday, the atmosphere will stay active enough to keep periodic shower and thunderstorm chances in the forecast through much of the week.

Severe Weather Threat Today

Showers and thunderstorms will continue developing throughout today as a very warm, humid, and unstable air mass remains in place across the region. While morning storms and cloud cover may limit instability in some locations, any areas that experience breaks in the clouds could quickly destabilize and support stronger thunderstorms later this afternoon and evening.

The primary threats today include:

Damaging wind gusts up to 60 mph
Large hail
Torrential rainfall
Frequent lightning

The flooding threat may become just as impactful as the severe weather threat. Some storms will produce very heavy rainfall rates due to exceptionally high atmospheric moisture levels. Repeated thunderstorms moving over the same areas could quickly lead to flash flooding, especially across portions of central and south-central Missouri. Some isolated locations may receive several inches of rainfall in a short amount of time.

Tonight Into Tuesday Morning

Another organized cluster of thunderstorms is expected to develop tonight across Kansas and Nebraska before pushing east into Missouri and Illinois overnight into Tuesday morning.

This round of storms may become more organized and could produce:

Widespread damaging winds
Large hail
Torrential rainfall
Brief isolated tornadoes

Overnight severe weather events can be especially dangerous because storms often move in while people are sleeping. Residents across Missouri and Illinois should have multiple ways to receive weather alerts tonight into Tuesday morning.

Tuesday Severe Weather Outlook

Tuesday may bring another round of potentially strong to severe thunderstorms ahead of an advancing cold front. The greatest concern appears to focus across:

The St. Louis metro
Southeast Missouri
Southern Illinois
Areas south and east of Interstate 70

If enough sunshine develops after the morning storms move through, the atmosphere could rapidly recover and become favorable for organized severe thunderstorms by Tuesday afternoon and evening.

All modes of severe weather may be possible Tuesday including:

Damaging straight-line winds
Large hail
Isolated tornadoes

Outflow boundaries left behind from earlier storms may also serve as focal points for stronger rotating thunderstorms.

Midweek and Late Week Outlook

While temperatures will cool noticeably behind Tuesday night’s cold front, the overall weather pattern may remain somewhat unsettled through the remainder of the week. Additional weak disturbances moving through the Midwest could keep occasional showers and thunderstorms in the forecast Wednesday through Friday, although widespread severe weather appears less likely after Tuesday.

Clouds, scattered showers, and cooler temperatures may dominate much of midweek with highs falling back below average for late May. By late week into next weekend, temperatures should gradually moderate once again, with additional storm chances potentially returning as the active spring pattern continues across the central United States.
